evals.name: json-parser-failure-empty-results
evals.prompt: I'm querying Sonarr JSON logs in Loki to find all lines where the HTTP response time exceeds 2 seconds. My current query is: {container="sonarr"} | json | response_time > 2s — but I'm getting empty results and I see __error__ labels appearing. What's wrong and how do I fix it?
evals.expected_output: The empty results are caused by parser failures: lines that fail `| json` parsing produce `__error__` labels and are excluded from downstream label filters, so the `response_time > 2s` filter sees nothing. Debug by adding `| __error__ != ""` after the parser to inspect failing lines — common causes are startup banners or non-JSON lines mixed into the stream. Fix by adding `|= "{"` as a line filter before `| json` to pre-filter to JSON-only lines, then extract only the needed field with `| json response_time` rather than parsing all fields.
expectations: Identifies __error__ labels as the signal that parser failures are silently dropping lines from downstream filters
Recommends `| __error__ != ""` as the diagnostic step to inspect failing lines
Recommends adding `|= "{"` as a line filter before `| json` to exclude non-JSON lines (startup banners, mixed formats)
Recommends extracting only the needed field with `| json response_time` rather than bare `| json`
Places the line filter (`|= "{"`) before the parser, not after, consistent with the skill's pipeline ordering rule

evals.name: per-container-error-rate-dashboard
evals.prompt: I want a LogQL metric query for a Grafana dashboard panel showing the per-minute error rate for each container on my homelab host, with the top 5 containers highlighted. The query is timing out. How should I write this correctly and efficiently?
evals.expected_output: Use `topk(5, sum by (container) (rate({host="homelab"} |= "error" [1m])))` — the `|= "error"` line filter runs as a distributed grep before any parsing, which is the cheapest filter and dramatically reduces data volume before aggregation. Avoid grouping `by(container)` on a high-cardinality label like request IDs or IPs; `container` is acceptable here. If the query still times out, add a more specific exact-match label to the stream selector (e.g., `compose_service`) to narrow the index lookup, and consider a recording rule for a frequently-loaded dashboard panel.
expectations: Places `|= "error"` as a line filter before any parser stage, not after, citing that line filters are the cheapest distributed grep
Uses `topk(5, ...)` wrapping `sum by (container)` matching the skill's example pattern exactly
Includes at least one exact-match label in the stream selector and explains this is the primary index lookup
Warns against grouping by high-cardinality labels (IPs, user IDs, request IDs) as a timeout/performance cause
Mentions recording rules as the fix for frequently-loaded dashboard panels

evals.name: correlate-activity-across-services
evals.prompt: Radarr added a movie but I can't tell if Prowlarr actually searched for it or if the download got stuck in qBittorrent. I don't have distributed tracing set up. How do I correlate activity across these three services in Loki?
evals.expected_output: Since off-the-shelf containers like Radarr, Prowlarr, and qBittorrent have no OpenTelemetry instrumentation, use shared domain identifiers as the homelab substitute for distributed tracing. Query `{container=~"radarr|prowlarr|qbittorrent"} |= "<tmdb-or-download-hash>"` to pull all log lines across services that reference the same movie or download. Use the TMDB ID if you know it (`|= "tmdbId=12345"`), or the download hash once Radarr hands off to qBittorrent. The `|~` operator can do a case-insensitive title search (`|~ "(?i)inception"`) but is slower — prefer an exact `|=` match on an ID when available.
expectations: Explicitly names the lack of OpenTelemetry instrumentation in off-the-shelf containers as the reason standard distributed tracing is unavailable
Recommends correlating by shared domain identifiers (TMDB ID, download hash, NZB ID) as the homelab substitute for distributed traces
Uses `container=~"radarr|prowlarr|qbittorrent"` with a regex stream selector to span multiple services in one query
Prefers `|=` exact-match line filter on an ID over `|~` regex for performance, explaining `|~` is slower
Avoids recommending OpenTelemetry instrumentation or trace injection as a solution for these containers
